God opens Ezekiel's eyes to something most people never see: the hidden corruption of His own people. While the elders of Judah sit with the prophet in Jerusalem, seemingly engaged in normal leadership, God seizes Ezekiel by the hair and tears him away into a vision of the temple. What unfolds is not a gradual moral decline but systematic, deliberate apostasy. Behind sealed walls, the very leaders of Israel are bowing before carved creatures and detestable beasts. They are not ignorant or confused—they are hiding. They believe God cannot see them. They are committing idolatry in darkness, each elder at his own shrine, convinced that "the LORD has forsaken the land." This is the lie that justifies everything. If God has abandoned them, then nothing matters. No covenant binds them. No law restrains them. They are free to worship whatever they choose.

What strikes deepest about this passage is how close the abominations are to the very presence of God. These are not backsliders worshiping in distant villages. Women weep for Tammuz at the north gate of the Lord's own house. Twenty-five men stand between the portico and the altar—at the heart of true worship—and turn their backs on God to bow toward the rising sun. They practice this desecration not once but routinely. The idolatry is not hidden shame but calculated defiance, done repeatedly in the place where God has promised His Name will dwell. What makes it worse is that these are leaders—the seventy elders of Israel, men supposed to guard what is holy. They have instead become priests of deception, leading God's people deeper into the darkness they themselves have chosen.

God shows Ezekiel layer upon layer of corruption, each revelation punctuated with the same terrible refrain: "Yet you will see even greater abominations." This is not mercy announcing forgiveness. This is the God of Israel preparing His prophet for judgment. God does not call Ezekiel to these visions to feel superior or to enjoy the spectacle of others' sin. He calls him to witness the full weight of what His people have become so that when judgment comes, it will not catch anyone unaware. God's response is final: "I will respond with wrath. I will not look on them with pity, nor will I spare them. Although they shout loudly in My ears, I will not listen to them." This is not the language of a God who has compromised or withdrawn. This is a God whose patience has reached its limit with a people who have replaced His truth with lies, His worship with idolatry, and His presence with pretense. The question Ezekiel's vision leaves with every reader is piercing: What are we hiding from God? What do we tell ourselves when we turn our backs on Him?
